Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty loose. I'd put in a decent amount of hours each week, maybe around 30 or so, but it wasn't like I had a strict schedule. I'd usually study when I felt like it or when something was due soon. Being a visual learner, I'd spend a lot of time with diagrams and charts, but I wasn't the type to plan out every study session in advance.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was going on. If there was a deadline coming up, I'd cram more hours in, but otherwise I'd just drift through the week without much structure. Some weeks I'd be pretty consistent, other weeks I'd barely touch anything until the last minute. It really depended on my mood and what else was happening in my life.
Q3: Usually it was because I had to check if something was due or if there was an announcement I might have missed. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there was anything new, but honestly, I wasn't super proactive about it. Deadlines were the main thing that got me to actually open the LMS. If nothing was urgent, I might not log in for days.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. It never felt like a habit I naturally developed. There were maybe a few weeks where I'd check in more regularly because assignments were piling up, but once those passed, I'd fall back into just logging in when I absolutely had to. The LMS never became something I wanted to use daily.
Q5: The video content was probably the most useful for me since I learn better visually. I could pause, rewind, and really look at things until they made sense. Some of the diagrams and visual resources helped a lot too. The discussion posts weren't really my thing, but I'd skim them occasionally to see if anyone had insights I missed.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums most of the time. I only posted when it was required, and even then I kept it minimal. Reading through long text-based resources felt like a chore, so I'd skip those unless I absolutely needed them for an assignment. If something wasn't directly tied to my grade, I probably didn't bother with it.
Q7: If it was going to help me pass or understand something I was stuck on, then it felt worth my time. Visual stuff like videos or infographics always felt more valuable to me than reading walls of text. Also, if I knew it would show up on an exam, I'd pay more attention. Otherwise, I'd just move on to something else.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt completely skippable to me. If it wasn't required, I probably wasn't doing it unless I was genuinely curious, which wasn't often. Required activities at least had some urgency to them, so I'd make sure to complete those. Optional stuff just felt like extra work when I already had enough on my plate.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download slides and videos and study them offline because my internet isn't always reliable and I don't have a lot of resources to keep streaming things. I'd also use my own notes and sometimes look stuff up on YouTube if the course materials weren't clicking. A lot of my actual studying happened away from the LMS.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be going over my handwritten notes or reviewing downloaded materials for hours, but none of that would show up online. I'd also study with a friend from my extracurricular group sometimes, and we'd work through problems together offline. The LMS would just show me as inactive even though I was actually learning.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, download it or take screenshots of key parts, and then review those offline later. It saved data and let me study whenever I wanted without needing to be connected. So my LMS activity looked way lower than my actual engagement with the material.
Q12: Around midterms things got rough. I had stuff going on with my extracurriculars and some personal stress that made it hard to focus. Even though I'm pretty resilient, there were moments where I just couldn't bring myself to care about logging in. The motivation would dip and I'd fall behind, then have to catch up later.
Q13: For sure. When I was tired or stressed, the last thing I wanted to do was navigate the LMS. If I had competing responsibilities, coursework would sometimes take a backseat. Confusion about assignments also made me avoid the platform because I didn't want to deal with figuring things out. I'd just wait until I absolutely had to.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd open materials and just zone out. I'd be watching a video but not really absorbing anything because my mind was elsewhere. Or I'd scroll through readings without actually processing the information. Technically I was "engaged" according to the system, but mentally I was checked out.
Q15: I'd put off discussion posts and longer readings pretty often. Even though I knew they mattered, they just felt tedious. I'd tell myself I'd do them later and then scramble at the last minute. Sometimes I avoided things because I wasn't confident I understood the material well enough to contribute meaningfully.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled everything. I'd barely touch the LMS until something was due soon, then I'd log in constantly to make sure I submitted on time. My activity would spike right before due dates and drop off immediately after. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged much at all.
Q17: Feedback helped when I actually got it in time to adjust. If I did poorly on something, I'd try to figure out what went wrong and engage more with those topics. But sometimes feedback came too late to really change how I approached the next thing. Quick feedback definitely motivated me more than waiting weeks for a grade.
Q18: The structure was okay, but it didn't really push me to stay consistent. If things were spread out evenly, I might have engaged more steadily. But when there were gaps without much due, I'd drift away. The peer support intervention helped a bit because having someone check in made me feel more accountable.
Q19: Having clear visual materials made engagement easier for me. But when there was too much reading or the navigation was confusing, I'd just give up and figure it out later. If the course had more interactive visual content, I probably would have stayed more engaged. The text-heavy parts were harder for me to get through.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for answers or trying to understand something but never actually completing the work. Or they could be like me, downloading stuff to use offline and then not submitting through the platform. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes you're just clicking around lost.
Q21: That's pretty much me. I didn't use the LMS that much, but I studied in other ways that worked better for me. I passed my exams and did okay on assignments even though my login numbers were low. The LMS isn't the only place learning happens, especially for someone with limited resources who studies offline a lot.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you actually understood anything. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with peers, the mental effort you put in away from the screen. It's just surface-level data that doesn't capture real learning.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's about being mentally present when you're studying, asking questions, and trying to connect ideas. You can be engaged without ever touching the LMS if you're genuinely thinking about the content.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied offline, the peer support conversations that helped me stay on track, or the times I was genuinely happy figuring something out on my own. It also wouldn't capture my resilience—how I bounced back after rough patches even when my activity looked low. There's a lot more to my engagement than clicks and logins.
